steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: Why do gases exert pressure on the walls of a container?
options: Because gas particles are heavy and press down due to gravity
Because gas particles collide with the walls and exert a force
Because gases always try to expand to fill the container
Because gas particles repel each other very strongly
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Gas pressure is caused by the collisions of gas particles with the container walls. Each collision exerts a tiny force, and the combined effect of billions of collisions per second creates pressure. It is not due to gravity or repulsion between particles.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Gas particles move randomly and collide with the container walls. The force from these collisions per unit area is the gas pressure.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.3.2 — Particle model and pressure

steps.title: The Particle Model
steps.bodyHTML: The particle model explains the properties of solids, liquids and gases in terms of the movement and arrangement of particles. Gas pressure arises from the collisions of gas particles with the container walls — increasing temperature increases the average kinetic energy of the particles, so they hit the walls more frequently and with greater force, raising the pressure. The particle model also explains why gases are compressible (large spaces between particles) while solids and liquids are not.

relationships: Gas pressure → caused by particle collisions with walls
Higher temperature → faster particles → greater pressure
Smaller volume → more frequent collisions → greater pressure
Gas compressible → large gaps between particles

commonErrors: Saying pressure is caused by particles pushing each other apart
Thinking cold gas particles are stationary
Forgetting that reducing volume increases pressure (more frequent collisions)

aqa.questionText: Explain, using the particle model, why the pressure of a gas in a sealed container increases when the temperature is increased.

steps.label: Link temperature to particle speed
steps.content: Increasing the temperature increases the average kinetic energy of the gas particles, so they move faster.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — higher temperature → greater kinetic energy / faster particles

steps.label: Link speed to collision rate
steps.content: Faster particles collide with the container walls more frequently.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — more frequent collisions with the walls

steps.label: Link collisions to pressure
steps.content: Each collision exerts a force on the wall; more frequent (and harder) collisions mean greater force per unit area, so greater pressure.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — greater force on walls / increased pressure

aqa.finalAnswer: Higher temperature → faster particles → more frequent, harder collisions with walls → greater pressure.

aqa.questionText: A gas is compressed into a smaller volume at constant temperature. What happens to the pressure?

options.label: A
options.text: Pressure decreases because particles slow down

options.label: B
options.text: Pressure stays the same because temperature is constant

options.label: C
options.text: Pressure increases because particles collide with the walls more often

options.label: D
options.text: Pressure decreases because there is less space between particles

markSchemeSteps: Smaller volume → particles travel shorter distances before hitting walls → more frequent collisions → higher pressure ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Option B ignores the effect of volume change — constant temperature means constant speed, but more frequent collisions still raise pressure.
